1: A type: the Shulammite is called out of her dancing to be seen — the invitation to appear before the bridegroom who celebrates her. Christ calls his church out of hiddenness and shame into the visibility of his own approval: 'You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden' (Matthew 5:14). The machola ha-machanayim (dance of two camps) invokes the Mahanaim of Gen 32 — the site of Jacob’s encounter with the angel-army of God. The beloved who dances between two heavenly camps is the church who belongs to two worlds — she dances at the intersection of earth and heaven, already held by the one who crossed from heaven to earth to find her.
2: A type: the bridegroom’s detailed description of the beloved’s body as the site of abundance — the navel like a bowl of wine, the belly like a mound of wheat — speaks to his delight in the whole person God has made. Christ’s love for the church is not a spiritual abstraction that ignores her embodied existence; it is the love of the Creator for what he made and declared good (Genesis 1:31). The body is the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19), and Christ’s declaration of the body’s beauty is the theological ground for bodily resurrection: the same body he declares beautiful, he will raise.
3: A type: the repeated twin-fawns image (cf. Song of Solomon 4:5) is the Song’s characteristic repetition — the same praise spoken again with fresh delight. Christ’s love for the church does not diminish through familiarity; it speaks the same declarations of beauty from generation to generation. The pastoral imagery (gazelle, twins, fawns) places the beloved in the created order that Christ made and entered: the Good Shepherd who knows his own (John 10:14) delights in them with the same fresh attentiveness as a shepherd delights in young animals in the spring.
4: A type: the church’s neck like an ivory tower speaks to the unyielding faithfulness of the church’s witness — the community that does not bend to cultural pressure. The eyes like the pools of Heshbon describe the depth and clarity of faith that sees clearly. Christ commissions this quality in his church: ‘let your eyes be healthy so that your whole body is full of light’ (Matthew 6:22). The nose facing Damascus — the city of Paul’s conversion and of the Gentile mission — is the prophetic posture of the church directed outward toward the world she is called to serve.
5: A type: the head like Carmel (the majestic promontory) and the flowing locks that capture the king speak to the church’s dignity and the paradoxical power of faith over Christ: he who ‘holds all things together’ (Colossians 1:17) is ‘captured’ by the church’s beauty — a beauty he himself gave her (Ephesians 5:26-27). This is the kenosis reversed: the one who emptied himself and humbled himself (Philippians 2:7-8) is now, from his position of exaltation, captivated by the church he redeemed. The purple locks (the royal color) placed on the beloved by the bridegroom’s love are the royal priesthood that Christ confers on the church (1 Peter 2:9).
6: A type: ‘How fair and pleasant you are, O love, with all your delights!’ is the summary declaration of the bridegroom’s delight in the beloved. Christ’s delight in the church is the love that preceded the cross — he endured the cross ‘for the joy set before him’ (Hebrews 12:2), and the church is part of that joy. Zephaniah 3:17 provides the fullest OT statement of this divine delight: ‘The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.’ The Song’s bridegroom-delight is the experiential image of the divine rejoicing-over that Zephaniah proclaims.
7: A type: the beloved’s stature like a date palm is the image of the church planted, rooted, and bearing fruit. Psalms 92:12 declares: ‘the righteous flourish like the palm tree.’ The church is the community of the righteous planted by Christ; her fruit clusters (Ephesians 5:9, the fruit of light) are what the Bridegroom comes to taste. The palm tree is also the tree of victory — the palm branches of the triumphal entry (John 12:13) and of the great multitude who stand before the throne (Revelation 7:9). The beloved-as-palm-tree is the church whose dignity Christ proclaims.
8: A type: ‘I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit-clusters.’ The bridegroom’s declaration of intentional pursuit is the Incarnation-movement translated into the language of love: Christ came to ‘lay hold’ of the church (Hebrews 2:16: ‘he does not lay hold of angels but of the offspring of Abraham’). The same Greek verb epilambanomai (to take hold of) appears in Hebrews 2:16 and describes precisely the incarnational reaching: God extended his hand into creation and took hold of human flesh in order to take hold of human persons. The climbing and reaching of Song of Solomon 7:8 is the poetry that Hebrews makes literal.
9: A type: the mouth of the beloved like the best wine going down smoothly — the reciprocity of love in which the church’s prayer is heard as the most exquisite speech by Christ, who desires to hear her voice (cf. Song of Solomon 2:14, ‘let me hear your voice, for your voice is sweet’). Christ invites the church into the communication of prayer: ‘ask, and it will be given to you’ (Matthew 7:7); ‘abide in me, and ask whatever you wish’ (John 15:7). The beloved’s voice that the bridegroom savors as wine is the church’s prayer received by Christ as the most precious thing she offers.
10: A direct revelation: ‘I am my beloved’s, and his desire is for me.’ The word teshuqah (desire/longing) here reverses Genesis 3:16’s distorted desire: where the fall introduced a desire entangled with domination, the Song restores the desire that is pure self-giving love. Christ’s desire for the church is the redemptive desire that is enacted in the cross: he desired to redeem what was lost, and ‘for the joy set before him he endured the cross’ (Hebrews 12:2). His desire brought him through death for the church. The beloved’s declaration ‘his desire is for me’ is the church’s assurance that she is the specific object of Christ’s redemptive longing — not an afterthought but the goal of the Incarnation.
11: A type: ‘Come, my beloved, let us go out into the fields.’ The beloved’s invitation to Christ to go with her into the world is the church’s missionary movement. The fields (sadeh) in the parable of the sower (Matthew 13:38) are the world; the church goes into that world not alone but with the Bridegroom: ‘Go therefore and make disciples of all nations… and behold, I am with you always’ (Matthew 28:19-20). The church’s invitation to Christ to come with her into the harvest-field is the prayer that precedes mission. The Lord of the harvest goes with the laborers he sends.
12: A type: the vineyard inspection — checking whether the vine has sprouted, the blossoms opened, the pomegranates flowered — is the church’s examination of her own fruitfulness before the return of the Bridegroom. Christ inspects the churches (Rev 2-3, the letters to the seven churches) with the same care the bridegroom brings to the vineyard: what is bearing fruit, what needs pruning, what has not yet bloomed. The beloved’s declaration ‘there I will give you my love’ is the church’s self-offering in the place of productive service — she gives herself most fully in the context of fruitfulness.
13: A type: the mandrakes giving fragrance and the precious fruits stored up — new and old — for the beloved represent the church’s accumulated offering to Christ. The early church leader who brings ‘out of his treasure what is new and what is old’ (Matthew 13:52) is the steward of the household of God who has stored both the ancient Scriptures and the new revelation of Christ. The church’s all-things-for-you is the participation in the Spirit’s work of preparing the Bride: ‘the Spirit and the Bride say, “Come”’ (Revelation 22:17) — and the Bride comes with her hands full of gifts for the Bridegroom.
